It grieves Solomon to see power prevail over what is right. Wherever we look, we see sad proof of the wickedness and misery of people, who try to cause trouble for themselves and each other. Being treated so harshly, people are tempted to hate and despise life. But a good person, even when badly off in this world, cannot have reason to wish he had never been born, since he is glorifying the Lord, even in suffering, and will be happy at last, forever happy. Unrighteous people have most reason to want life to continue with all its troubles, as a far more miserable condition awaits them if they die in their sins. If human and worldly things were our greatest good, not existing would be preferable to life, considering the various oppressions here below.

Solomon notices the sources of trouble specific to those who do well, and includes all who labor with diligence, and whose efforts are crowned with success. They often become great and prosperous, but this excites envy and opposition. Others, seeing the troubles of an active life, foolishly expect more satisfaction in laziness and idleness. But laziness is a sin that punishes itself. Let us by honest work secure what we need, so that we will not lack necessities, but not grasp at too much, which would only create trouble for our spirits. Moderate effort and gain are best.

Frequently, the more people have, the more they want; and on this they are so intent that they get no enjoyment from what they have. Selfishness is the cause of this evil. A selfish person cares for nobody; there is only himself to take care of, yet he will scarcely allow necessary rest to himself or the people he employs. He never thinks he has enough. He has enough for his work, for his family, but not enough for his eyes. Many are so focused on the world that in pursuit of it they deprive themselves, not only of God’s favor and eternal life, but also of the pleasures of this life. Distant relatives or strangers who inherit such a person's wealth never thank him. Covetousness grows stronger with time and habit; people tottering on the brink of death become more greedy. Sadly, how often do we see people professing to be followers of Him, "who though he was rich, for our sakes became poor," anxiously gathering money together and holding it fast, excusing themselves by common-place talking about the necessity of care, and the danger of extravagance!

Surely he has more satisfaction in life who labors hard to maintain those he loves than the miser has in his toil. In all things unity tends to success and safety, but above all, the union of Christians. They assist each other by encouragement or friendly reproof. They warm each other's hearts while they converse together about the love of Christ, or join in singing His praises. Then let us improve our opportunities for Christian fellowship. In these things all is not vanity, though there will be some imperfection as long as we are under the sun. Where two are closely joined in holy love and fellowship, Christ will by His Spirit come to them; then there is a threefold cord.

People are never long easy and satisfied; they are fond of changes. This is no new thing. Rulers see themselves slighted by those they have tried to please; this is vanity and trouble for the spirit. But the willing servants of the Lord Jesus, our King, rejoice in Him alone, and they will love Him more and more forever.
